source.zh: 胡安·德·贝坦索斯《印加人的总述与叙事》（1551）；佩德罗·谢萨·德莱昂《秘鲁编年史》第一部（1553）与第二部《印加的统治》（约1553年成书，1880年才刊行）；佩德罗·萨米恩托·德·甘博亚《印加史》（1572）；克里斯托瓦尔·德·莫利纳《印加神话与仪式记述》（约1575）；印卡·加西拉索·德拉维加《印卡王室述评》（1609）；贝尔纳贝·科博《新世界史》（1653）；费利佩·瓜曼·波马·德·阿亚拉《新编年史与善政》（约1615）；克丘亚语文书《瓦罗奇里手稿》（约1608）
source.en: Juan de Betanzos, Suma y narración de los Incas (1551); Pedro Cieza de León, Crónica del Perú, Part One (1553) and Part Two, El señorío de los Incas (written c. 1553, unpublished until 1880); Pedro Sarmiento de Gamboa, Historia de los Incas (1572); Cristóbal de Molina, Relación de las fábulas y ritos de los Incas (c. 1575); Inca Garcilaso de la Vega, Comentarios reales de los Incas (1609); Bernabé Cobo, Historia del Nuevo Mundo (1653); Felipe Guaman Poma de Ayala, Nueva corónica y buen gobierno (c. 1615); the Quechua-language Huarochirí Manuscript (c. 1608)

note.zh:
印加人没有文字，奇普（结绳）主要用于记数，能否记录叙事至今有争议。因此这条时间线上的每一句话都来自1532年征服之后的西班牙文文献：写它们的是士兵、教士和王室特使，材料靠翻译从库斯科的贵族口中转录，动机各不相同。萨米恩托1572年那部是奉总督托莱多之命编的，目的正是论证印加人是晚近的篡夺者，以此为西班牙统治张目；加西拉索1609年在西班牙落笔，凭童年记忆加人文主义的书本学问，把印加写成一个仁德的开化王朝；谢萨的第二部写于1553年前后，压了三百多年才刊行；莫利纳那部只靠一部抄本传下来。

各家讲的并不是一个故事。造世造了几次就不一致：贝坦索斯写维拉科查两次出湖；萨米恩托写的是两轮创造，但他笔下的巨人只是照身量试造的样子，真正被“乌努·帕查库提”毁掉的，是随后照造物主自己身量造出来的那一批人——流行的复述常把这两截并成“一代巨人被洪水灭掉”，原文并不这么说；另有一些记述根本没有巨人这一节。第一代人是被洪水淹死还是被变成石头，同一部书里往往并存。两人连维拉科查从湖中现身的时点都不一样：贝坦索斯排在造人之前，萨米恩托排在洪水之后。下面按贝坦索斯与萨米恩托的骨架排列，冲突处在正文里直接点出。

维拉科查是否是凌驾于太阳之上的至高创世神，学界一直存疑。帝国的国家祭祀是太阳的（科里坎查、因蒂·拉伊米），“看不见的至高造物主”这个位置有多少是编年史家按一神教框架整理出来的，很难判断；那位白袍、白须、拄杖游走的老者，殖民时代的作者曾直接比附为使徒圣托马斯或圣巴托洛梅，这类描写有多少出自原住民口传也说不清。创世现场同样是政治的：蒂瓦纳科在印加人征服科亚苏尤时已经是废墟几百年了（蒂瓦纳科文明约兴盛于公元500至1000年），的的喀喀湖一带原本是艾马拉人的地方，当地的图努帕信仰在殖民文献里与维拉科查混成一体。

还有三点要交代。一、安第斯的开端没有虚无，也没有开天辟地：大地本来就在，只是黑着——缺这一段本身是信息。二、曼科·卡帕克建库斯科属于王朝起源传说，和维拉科查创世本是两套叙事，是编年史家把它们接在一起的；这套传说自身还有两个互斥的版本（的的喀喀湖降临持金杖／帕卡里坦博三窗山洞走出八兄妹）。三、古典文献里没有世界末日：帕查库提指的是天翻地覆的更替而非终结，瓜曼·波马开列的五个世代已经被他嵌进了圣经史框架，印卡里则是征服之后才长出来的传说，20世纪50年代才被采录成文。

作为对照：唯一一部早期用克丘亚语写成的长篇文书《瓦罗奇里手稿》里也有洪水，但那是瓦罗奇里山区的传统，不是库斯科的——一头美洲驼不肯吃草，告诉主人五天后海水要漫上来，两人爬上维尔卡科托山，各种野兽都挤在那里，狐狸的尾巴垂下去沾了水，从此尖端发黑。这个故事与上面这条线并不属于同一个体系，不能混为一谈。
note.en:
The Incas had no writing. Khipu, the knotted cords, were used above all for counting, and whether they carried narrative is still argued. Every sentence in this timeline therefore comes from Spanish-language texts written after the conquest of 1532, by soldiers, priests and royal commissioners, taken down through interpreters from Cusco noblemen, and each with its own agenda. Sarmiento wrote in 1572 on the orders of Viceroy Toledo, whose purpose was to show that the Incas were recent usurpers and Spanish rule therefore legitimate. Garcilaso wrote in Spain in 1609 out of childhood memory and humanist reading, and produced a benign, civilizing dynasty. Cieza's second part sat in manuscript for three centuries. Molina's account survives in a single copy.

The chroniclers do not tell one story. They disagree on how many creations there were: Betanzos has Viracocha come out of the lake twice; Sarmiento has two rounds, but his giants are only trial models made to test the size, and the people the unu pachacuti destroys are the ordinary-sized ones made afterwards in the maker's own likeness — popular retellings collapse the two into a race of giants drowned by the flood, which is not what the text says; other accounts have no giants at all. Whether the first people were drowned or petrified is often left standing both ways within a single book. The two men do not even place Viracocha's emergence from the lake at the same point: Betanzos puts it before the making of people, Sarmiento after the flood. The sequence below follows the Betanzos-Sarmiento spine, and the conflicts are flagged in the stages rather than smoothed over.

Whether Viracocha really stood above the Sun as a supreme creator is unsettled. The state cult of the empire was the Sun's — the Coricancha, Inti Raymi — and how much of the invisible supreme maker is the work of chroniclers reaching for a monotheistic frame is hard to say. Colonial writers went so far as to identify the bearded old wanderer in the white robe with the Apostle Thomas or Bartholomew, which leaves the white robe and the beard themselves under suspicion. The setting is political too: Tiahuanaco had been a ruin for centuries when the Incas took the Collao (the Tiwanaku culture flourished roughly 500-1000 CE), the Titicaca basin was Aymara country, and the local Tunupa was blended into Viracocha in the colonial sources.

Three more things. There is no void here and no parting of sky and earth: the land is already present, merely dark, and that absence is itself information. The founding of Cusco by Manco Cápac is a dynastic charter, a separate cycle that the chroniclers spliced onto the creation, and it comes in two irreconcilable versions of its own (descent at Lake Titicaca with a golden rod; eight siblings out of the three-windowed cave at Pacaritambo). And there is no end of the world in the early record: pachakuti means an overturning, a turn of the age, not a finish. Guaman Poma's five generations of humankind are already fitted into biblical history, and Inkarri is a post-conquest tradition, first written down in the 1950s.

For comparison: the Huarochirí Manuscript, the one substantial early text composed in Quechua, also has a flood, but it belongs to the Huarochirí highlands and not to Cusco. A llama refuses to eat and tells its owner that in five days the sea will rise; the two climb the mountain Huillcacoto, where every animal is already crowded together, and the fox's tail hangs down into the water, which is why its tip is black. It is a different tradition and should not be folded into the one below.

title.en: The land was there, and it was night

text.zh: 贝坦索斯说，秘鲁这片土地起初是一场长夜：山川已经在那里，只是没有太阳，没有白昼。地上已经住着人，也有一位管着他们的君长，但讲述的人早已记不得那批人叫什么、那位君长叫什么。萨米恩托从另一头说同一件事：造物主先造出了世界，而造出来的世界是暗的。
text.en: Betanzos says the land of Peru was at first one long night: the country was already there, but there was no sun and no day. People lived on it, and they had a lord who commanded them, but the men who told him the story no longer remembered the name of that people or the name of that lord. Sarmiento says the same thing from the other end: the Creator made the world, and the world he made was dark.

title.zh: 孔·蒂西·维拉科查出于湖中
title.en: Con Tici Viracocha comes out of the lake

text.zh: 一位君长从科亚苏尤的一座湖里出来，编年史家指认那是的的喀喀湖。贝坦索斯记他的名字叫孔·蒂西·维拉科查，说他带着一批随从上岸，在湖畔的蒂瓦纳科落脚。萨米恩托称他维拉科查·帕查亚查奇，意思是“教诲世界的人”，并留下一副像貌：中等身量，肤色白，穿一件束腰的白袍，手里拿着一根杖和一本书。萨米恩托的次序与贝坦索斯不同，他把维拉科查在的的喀喀湖现身排在下面那场洪水之后。
text.en: A lord came up out of a lake in Collasuyu, which the chroniclers take to be Titicaca. Betanzos gives his name as Con Tici Viracocha, and has him come ashore with a company of attendants and stop at Tiahuanaco on the lakeside. Sarmiento calls him Viracocha Pachayachachi, the teacher of the world, and leaves a description: a man of middle height, white, in a white belted robe, with a staff in one hand and a book in the other. Sarmiento's order differs from Betanzos's — he places the appearance at Titicaca after the flood described below.

title.zh: 先造巨人，再照自己的身量造人
title.en: Giants first, then men his own size

text.zh: 萨米恩托说，世界还黑着的时候，维拉科查先刻画出一批巨人，大得不成比例，是要看看照这个身量造人合不合适。他看着不妥，改按自己的身量来造，于是有了第一批人，个头与今人无异，活在没有太阳的地上。他给他们立下一条戒律，吩咐彼此不要相争、要认他侍奉他；那条戒律到底是什么，萨米恩托说讲述的人已经说不上来了。他们守了一阵，后来起了骄横与贪心，把戒律破了。贝坦索斯的版本里没有巨人这一节：黑暗中原本就住着的那批人触怒了造物主。
text.en: While the world was still dark, Sarmiento says, Viracocha first made giants — painted and sculptured, out of all proportion in their size — to see whether men of that size would do. He judged that they would not, and made people of his own stature instead: the first men, the size men are now, living on an earth with no sun above it. He gave them a precept to keep and told them to live without quarrelling and to serve him; what the precept was, Sarmiento says, the men who told him the story could no longer say. They kept it for a time, then fell into pride and greed and broke it. Betanzos has no giants at all: the people already living in the dark angered their maker.

title.zh: 乌努·帕查库提：翻覆大地的水
title.en: Unu pachacuti, the water that overturns the land

text.zh: 维拉科查降罚：一部分人被变成石头，一部分变作别的东西，一部分被大地和海水吞下去，其余的被一场大水淹没。萨米恩托把这场洪水记作克丘亚语的“乌努·帕查库提”，并译作“翻覆大地的水”，说雨下了六十天六夜，凡是造出来的都淹死了；那些受罚化成石头的，还留了些痕迹在离库斯科六十里格的普卡拉建筑里，算是给后人看的样子。谢萨·德莱昂在蒂瓦纳科听当地人讲，那片巨石建筑远在印加人之前就立在那里，是一夜之间起来的。
text.en: Viracocha cursed them. Some were turned to stone, some into other things, some were swallowed by the earth and some by the sea, and over the rest he sent a general flood, which Sarmiento sets down in Quechua as unu pachacuti and glosses as the water that overturns the land. It rained sixty days and sixty nights, he says, and everything made was drowned; of those turned to stone some vestiges were left in the buildings at Pucara, sixty leagues from Cusco, as a memorial and an example to posterity. At Tiahuanaco, Cieza de León was told that the great stonework had stood there long before the Incas and had gone up in a single night.

title.en: Sun, moon and stars rise out of the lake

text.zh: 水退之后，萨米恩托说，维拉科查留下三个人帮他造新的人，其中一个叫塔瓜帕卡。他到的的喀喀湖边，令太阳从湖中升起，又令月亮与星辰跟着出来，给太阳定下每日要走的路线，日子从此可以计数。月亮起初造得比太阳还亮，太阳嫉妒，趁两人一同升上天时抓一把炉灰掷在她脸上，月色从此就暗了下来。塔瓜帕卡在这时不听号令，被捆住手脚放上一张筏子推进湖里，一路咒骂，扬言还要回来报复，顺着湖水的出口漂走，此后不知所终。维拉科查在那地方立起一尊圣像，标明他在此造物；科博记下的太阳岛圣岩蒂蒂卡拉，后来一直被指认为太阳出来的地方。
text.en: When the water had gone down, Sarmiento says, Viracocha kept three men to help him make the new people, one of them called Taguapaca. At Lake Titicaca he commanded the sun to come up out of the water, and the moon and the stars to follow it, and he fixed the course the sun was to run each day; from then on the days could be counted. The moon was made brighter than the sun, and the sun in his envy flung a handful of ashes in her face as the two of them rose, and she has worn the darkened face ever since. Taguapaca, at this point, would not do as he was told: he was bound hand and foot, set on a balsa and pushed out into the lake, cursing and swearing he would come back and take his revenge, and the water carried him off down the outlet and no one knew where he went. Viracocha set up a holy image there to mark what he had made; the Titikala, the sacred rock on the Island of the Sun that Cobo describes, was afterwards pointed out as the place the sun came from.

title.zh: 以石刻人，令其出于洞穴与泉眼
title.en: Cut in stone, called up out of caves and springs

text.zh: 维拉科查在蒂瓦纳科用石头刻出各族人的样子：每族一位头人，还有怀孕的妇人、摇篮里的婴儿。他给每一族画好该穿的衣裳、该留的发式，分派语言、歌谣和随身要带的种子。刻完，他命这些人钻进地下，各自从他指定的地方冒出来——洞穴、泉眼、河源、山岩。那处出生地在克丘亚语里叫帕卡里纳，此后一直是本族祭拜的圣物。莫利纳把随行的两位记成维拉科查的儿子：伊迈马纳·维拉科查取道东边的安第斯山林，托卡波·维拉科查取道西边的海岸低地，沿路按名呼唤各族出土，一并给沿途的树木花草果实起名，指明哪些能吃、哪些能治病；维拉科查自己走中间的高原大道北行。
text.en: At Tiahuanaco he cut the nations out of stone: a headman for each people, women big with child, babies in their cradles. He painted on them the clothes each nation was to wear and the way each was to cut its hair, and dealt out to each its language, its songs and the seeds it was to carry. Then he sent them down under the earth to come up at the places he had named — a cave, a spring, the head of a river, a face of rock. That birthplace is the pacarina, and each people kept it afterwards as a shrine. Molina makes the two who travelled with him Viracocha's sons: Imaymana Viracocha took the road east through the Andean forests and Tocapo Viracocha the road west through the coastal plains, calling each nation up out of the ground by name and naming the trees and flowers and fruits as they went, telling which could be eaten and which would cure. Viracocha himself took the highland road up the middle, going north.

title.en: Fire at Cacha, then out across the sea

text.zh: 走到卡查（今拉克奇），当地的卡纳人持械围上来。他唤下天火烧山，卡纳人扔了兵器伏地请罪，他用杖把火打灭；被烧过的浮石至今散在那片地面上，拉克奇的维拉科查神庙就立在旁边。他一路向北，最后到厄瓜多尔海岸的曼塔，与随从一同踏着海面向西去，再没回来。从此造物主不在场：人祭拜的是脚下的帕查玛玛、头顶的雪峰阿普、本族的帕卡里纳。
text.en: At Cacha, now Raqchi, the Cana people came at him with weapons. He called fire down on the hill; they threw down their arms and fell on their faces, and he beat the fire out with his staff. The scorched stone is light enough to float and still lies scattered over that ground, with the Temple of Wiracocha standing beside it. He went on north to Manta on the coast of Ecuador and walked out over the sea toward the west with his companions, and did not come back. The maker is absent after that. What people attend to is Pachamama underfoot, the snow peaks that are the apus, and their own pacarina.

title.zh: 金杖没入库斯科的土地
title.en: The golden rod sinks into the ground at Cusco

text.zh: 王朝的开端接在这里。加西拉索听来的王室口传说：太阳神因蒂派儿子曼科·卡帕克与女儿妈妈奥克略从的的喀喀湖出发，交给他们一根金杖，杖一插而没之处便是建城之地。金杖在库斯科谷地的瓦纳考里山没入土中，库斯科由此奠基，太阳的居所因蒂坎查（后来的科里坎查）立在城心。贝坦索斯与萨米恩托讲的是另一头：四兄四妹从帕卡里坦博的三窗山洞走出，一路走到库斯科。两说不能合并。西班牙征服之后，安第斯乡间又长出印卡里传说——被斩首的印加王，头颅埋在地下，正朝着躯体重新长回去。
text.en: The dynasty begins here. In the royal tradition Garcilaso heard as a boy, the sun god Inti sent down his son Manco Cápac and his daughter Mama Ocllo at Lake Titicaca with a golden rod, telling them to travel until it sank into the earth at one thrust: there they were to build. It went into the ground at Huanacauri in the valley of Cusco, and Cusco was founded, with the Inticancha, the house of the Sun that became the Coricancha, at its heart. Betanzos and Sarmiento tell it from the other end: four brothers and four sisters walked out of the three-windowed cave at Pacaritambo and made their way to Cusco. The two accounts cannot be reconciled. After the Spanish conquest a further story grew up in the Andean countryside: the beheaded Inca, his head buried in the earth, growing back toward his body.
